summary: Gruber examines the controversy over Apple pressuring Telegram to remove posts by Belarusian pro-democracy protesters that revealed personal information about those who rigged the election for Lukashenko. While acknowledging the complexity of Apple policing third-party social networks, he zeroes in on a simpler issue: Apple's pattern of forbidding apps from explaining App Store rules to their own users, labeling such explanations as 'irrelevant.' He connects this to Apple's similar blocking of Facebook from telling users about Apple's 30% fee. Gruber argues this is not about relevance but about Apple's convenience, and that prohibiting apps from explaining the rules they operate under is fundamentally indefensible.
key_points: Apple asked Telegram to remove specific posts by Belarusian pro-democracy activists, citing App Store rules about personal information in user-generated content
Durov argues that while Apple frames it as removing individual posts, those posts are the entire purpose of the channels, making it effectively a channel shutdown
Apple previously told Telegram it cannot even show users a notice explaining why content was removed, calling such notices 'irrelevant'
Gruber draws a direct parallel to Apple blocking Facebook from informing users about the 30% App Store fee on online events
Gruber's core argument: it is prima facie wrong that App Store rules prohibit apps from explaining App Store rules to users
money_quotes: This has nothing to do with relevance and everything to do with convenience.
It is prima facie wrong that one of the rules of the App Store is that an app is not allowed to explain the rules of the App Store.
I'm hard pressed to think of an exception to this conviction, not just on Apple's App Store, but in any sphere of life — whether a harmless game or the administration of the law.
I think the reason certain content was censored or why the price is 30% higher is the opposite of irrelevant.
This whole issue of Apple injecting itself into the internal policing of a third-party social network is complicated, to say the least.
key_insight: Apple's prohibition against apps explaining App Store rules to their own users is indefensible in any context, and its use of 'irrelevance' as justification is really about shielding itself from inconvenient transparency.
